author: @K9JT
text: So I see you tried your hand at making some music...better stick to what you're good at Rob, haha :-)

author: @roidroid
text:
Rob i just realised you could make a coil *very* quickly by drawing a long straight line of the conductive ink onto a strip of paper, then rolling the paper into a tube.  Tada, there's your coil!

 #thinkingin3D  ;)

author: @roidroid
text:
you could probably fold the paper (or plastic) over onto itself to make even smaller multilayered/3D circuits.  Put a book ontop of it to keep the folds in position until it's dried.
If it's painted onto a plastic film, you could even put it into the microwave for a second or two to quickly weld the folds into position (thx to your ink).

replies.author: @Audabonify
replies.text:
This is a really interesting idea. You could weld the whole lot onto a thicker base plate so that the circuit had enough structural integrity for mounting.

I can imagine printing each layer on a film, cut out the layers and align them (or fold as you suggest), stick a base layer of plastic for integrity, and microwave them. The layers will be bonded by the via points and extra ink placed strategically for for structural integrity.

I guess the only issue here is how you create the vias between the layers. I guess this ink won't "flow" through holes? Perhaps for vias you could pin-prick a hole and then wet the ink again. I dunno, just some ideas.

author: @thagrit
text: Hey Robert , awesome job there. You haven't said anything more about your fuser. I think you should look at a channel called glass linger. He makes old school rca vacuum tubes.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hiya mate - i mention it in the homemade ink video - but that is a hobby and this is work - world of difference in priority there lol

text: Really cool. Now add this ink to a printhead on a 3d printer and a robot to place the smd components....

author: @MrFreddiew1
text: This is pretty cool Robert. I suppose you could for accuracy, design and print your circuit in fast draught for just getting the outline, and then draw over the print with the conductive ink...Just an idea.
